title: End of module: Nuclear Physics and Radioactivity

questions.text: In the nuclide notation $\,{}^{A}_{Z}X$, what does $Z$ represent?
choices: The mass number (total number of nucleons)
The number of protons
The number of neutrons
The number of electrons in the neutral atom's outer shell
questions.explanation: $Z$ is the proton number: it fixes which element the nuclide is. $A$ (mass number) is the total number of nucleons (protons + neutrons), and the number of neutrons is $N = A - Z$.

questions.text: How many neutrons are there in a nucleus of ${}^{235}_{92}\text{U}$?
What name is given to atoms of the same element (the same proton number) that have different numbers of neutrons, and hence different mass numbers?
questions.explanation: These are called **isotopes**: same $Z$ (same chemical element), different $N$ and hence different $A$. Carbon-12 and carbon-14 are isotopes of carbon.

questions.text: Which particle is emitted in alpha ($\alpha$) decay?
choices: A single proton
An electron
A helium nucleus (2 protons + 2 neutrons)
A high-energy photon
questions.explanation: An alpha particle is a helium-4 nucleus, ${}^{4}_{2}\text{He}$, ejected from the parent nucleus. It reduces the daughter's mass number by 4 and proton number by 2.

questions.text: Which particle completes this alpha-decay equation? $\;{}^{226}_{88}\text{Ra} \rightarrow {}^{222}_{86}\text{Rn} + \,?$
choices: ${}^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ (an alpha particle)
${}^{0}_{-1}\text{e}$ (a beta-minus particle)
${}^{1}_{0}\text{n}$ (a neutron)
A gamma-ray photon
questions.explanation: Mass number falls by $226 - 222 = 4$ and proton number falls by $88 - 86 = 2$ — exactly what an alpha particle, ${}^{4}_{2}\text{He}$, carries away. Check: $226 = 222 + 4$ and $88 = 86 + 2$.

questions.text: In beta-minus ($\beta^-$) decay, how do the mass number $A$ and proton number $Z$ of the nucleus change?
choices: $A$ decreases by 4, $Z$ decreases by 2
$A$ is unchanged, $Z$ increases by 1
$A$ is unchanged, $Z$ decreases by 1
$A$ and $Z$ are both unchanged
questions.explanation: In $\beta^-$ decay a neutron converts into a proton, an electron and an antineutrino: ${}^{A}_{Z}X \rightarrow {}^{A}_{Z+1}Y + {}^{0}_{-1}\text{e} + \bar{\nu}_e$. The nucleon count ($A$) is unchanged since a neutron simply became a proton; $Z$ increases by 1 because there is now one more proton.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about gamma ($\gamma$) radiation are correct?
choices: It is the most ionising of the three radiation types
It is the most penetrating of the three radiation types
It is uncharged
It is deflected by a magnetic field
questions.explanation: Gamma radiation is uncharged electromagnetic radiation. Being uncharged, it interacts weakly with matter, so it is the **least** ionising but the **most penetrating** of the three types (needing thick lead or concrete to attenuate it substantially), and — having no charge — it is not deflected by a magnetic (or electric) field.

questions.text: Which statement best describes the nature of radioactive decay?
choices: It can be sped up by heating or compressing the sample
It is a random, spontaneous process, unaffected by temperature, pressure or chemical state
Every nucleus in a sample decays at exactly the same instant
It only occurs inside a nuclear reactor
questions.explanation: Radioactive decay is spontaneous (triggered by nothing external) and random (no way to predict which nucleus decays, or exactly when). It is completely unaffected by temperature, pressure, or the chemical/physical environment of the nucleus.

questions.text: A radioactive isotope has decay constant $\lambda = 4.95\times10^{-2}\,\text{day}^{-1}$. Calculate its half-life, in days.
Iodine-131 has a half-life of $8.0$ days. Calculate its decay constant, in $\text{s}^{-1}$.
questions.explanation: Convert the half-life to seconds: $T_{1/2} = 8.0 \times 86400 = 691200\,\text{s}$. Then $\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \dfrac{0.6931}{691200} = 1.00\times10^{-6}\,\text{s}^{-1}$.

questions.text: A sample contains $5.0\times10^{12}$ atoms of a radioisotope with decay constant $2.5\times10^{-4}\,\text{s}^{-1}$. Calculate the initial activity of the sample, in Bq.
A sample initially contains $N_0 = 8.0\times10^{10}$ nuclei of a radioisotope with half-life $3.0$ hours. How many nuclei remain after $9.0$ hours?
questions.explanation: $9.0$ hours is exactly $9.0/3.0 = 3$ half-lives, so $N = N_0 \left(\tfrac12\right)^3 = \dfrac{8.0\times10^{10}}{8} = 1.0\times10^{10}$ nuclei.

questions.text: The mass defect of a helium-4 nucleus is $0.0304\,\text{u}$. Using $1\,\text{u} = 931.5\,\text{MeV}/c^2$, calculate its binding energy in MeV.
Calculate the energy released, in J, when a mass of $1.00\times10^{-5}\,\text{kg}$ is converted completely into energy. Use $c = 3.00\times10^{8}\,\text{m s}^{-1}$.
Which of the following statements about nuclear fission are correct?
choices: It is the splitting of a heavy nucleus into two lighter nuclei
The products have a greater binding energy per nucleon than the original nucleus
It is the process that releases energy inside the Sun
A chain reaction is possible because each fission event can release further neutrons
questions.explanation: Fission splits a heavy nucleus (e.g. uranium-235) into two lighter, more tightly bound daughter nuclei, releasing energy, and typically releases more than one neutron per event, which can trigger further fissions — a chain reaction. It is **fusion**, not fission, that powers the Sun.

questions.text: Nuclear fusion releases energy for light nuclei because…
choices: the products have a lower binding energy per nucleon than the reactants
the products have a higher binding energy per nucleon than the reactants
mass is created out of pure energy
the strong nuclear force switches off at short range
questions.explanation: Light nuclei sit on the steeply rising part of the binding-energy-per-nucleon curve. Fusing them produces a nucleus further up the curve (higher $E_b/A$, more tightly bound), and the energy difference is released.
